blocks.body: Word Order: Common Mistakes focuses on typical breaks in SVO, adverb position, and word order in questions, negatives, and indirect questions.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"Mistakes with word order usually appear when a first-language pattern pushes harder than the English one. English depends on stable positions for the subject, verb, adverb, and auxiliary.","rules":[{"label":"SVO","color":"emerald","text":"In a normal statement, English prefers subject + verb + object.","example":"She opened the window."},{"label":"ADVERB PLACEMENT","color":"blue","text":"Adverbs of frequency often stand before the main verb, but after be.","example":"I usually work late. / He is always tired."},{"label":"QUESTIONS AND NEGATIVES","color":"amber","text":"Questions and negatives depend on auxiliaries: do, does, did, am, is, are, have, will and others.","example":"Why did she leave? / He does not agree."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Where Learners Most Often Get Lost
blocks.body:
English word order looks simple, but the same problems repeat again and again.

  • In statements, learners often move an adverb or object to the front for no clear reason: Always I forget...
  • In questions, they forget the auxiliary before the subject: Where you are going?
  • In negatives, they try to attach not directly to the main verb in simple tenses: He not likes...
  • In indirect questions, they keep the direct-question order instead of using statement order: Can you tell me where is the station?

blocks.heading: 2. How the Correct Pattern Works
blocks.body:
Keep a short checking chain in mind.

  1. Statement: who? does what? to what? -> My brother usually takes the bus.
  2. Question: wh-word or yes/no + auxiliary + subject + base form / participle -> Why did they cancel the lesson?
  3. Negative: subject + auxiliary + not + main verb -> We do not understand this rule yet.
  4. Indirect question: after Can you tell me..., I wonder..., or Do you know..., return to the normal order -> Do you know where the station is?

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Putting an adverb of frequency at the front for no reason.","wrong":"Always I forget my keys.","right":"I always forget my keys.","hint":"In a neutral sentence, always, usually, often often stand before the main verb."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Leaving a question without an auxiliary or with the wrong order.","wrong":"Where you are going?","right":"Where are you going?","hint":"In a direct question, the auxiliary stands before the subject."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Building a negative without do / does / did in simple tenses.","wrong":"He not likes spicy food.","right":"He does not like spicy food.","hint":"In the Present Simple and Past Simple, the negative needs an auxiliary, and the main verb returns to the base form."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Keeping inversion in an indirect question.","wrong":"Can you tell me where is the station?","right":"Can you tell me where the station is?","hint":"After the introductory phrase, the indirect question returns to statement order."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaways","items":["In a neutral statement, check subject + verb + object first.","Adverbs of frequency often stand before the main verb, but after be.","Do not forget the auxiliary in questions and negatives.","In indirect questions, move the sentence back to statement order after the opening phrase."]}
